title: Becoming an Angel: The Mundus Imaginalis and the Platonic Path of Self-Knowledge
author: Angela Voss

body.text: The paper begins with alchemy because alchemy, for Corbin, was the inner work of uniting the soul with its heavenly counterpart within the hidden ground that symbolic images point toward. This is the intermediate place between spirit and matter, the mundus imaginalis, where the spiritual world assumes objective reality and where the transmutation of the psyche into the subtle body becomes possible through an encounter with an angelic presence.

Alchemy for Corbin is essentially the inner, spiritual work of attaining union between the human soul and its heavenly counterpart within the mysterious ground of the 'hidden secret' pointed to by the symbolic image. This is the intermediate place between spirit and matter, the mundus imaginalis, where the spiritual world assumes an objective reality, and where the transmutation of the prima materia of the human psyche into the subtle or spiritual body is the work of an alchemical opus that involves encounter with an angelic presence through the faculty of the active imagination. In this paper, Iintend to explore the nature of this encounter in the context of the neoplatonism of the Islamic mystical philosophers to whom Corbin dedicated his life and work.

The distinction between Aristotelian and Platonic modes of knowing is essential here, because Corbin'sproject depends on the Platonic conviction that one must participate in areality in order to know it. The observer cannot stand apart from what he observes; he must enter into it and allow himself to be transformed by it. It is this participatory mode of knowing that separates the study of the imaginal world from living within it.

To begin, we should briefly consider the kind of knowledge that characterises the Platonic path of gnosis. One of the ways the Aristotelian and Platonic methods of philosophising were distinguished in the Renaissance was through the assignment of a 'human' mode of investigation to the former, and a 'divine' mode of revelation to the latter. Human modes were characterised by rational, theoretical and analytical attempts to grasp the world of nature through observation and deduction, whereas divine modes embodied adeep intuitive sense of transcendent principles governing and emanating throughout creation, apprehended only through the highest intellectual principle in the soul which recognised the images of its divine source. The former entailed the separation of the observer from the object observed, the latter direct participation in it in order to know it. The former took place in time, the latter in atimeless place beyond the working out of cause and effect. Such contrasting modes lie behind the statement of Henry Corbin that 'the Active Imagination is not atheory, it is an initiation to vision.'

Iamblichus is the crucial figure here, because he articulated the distinction between talking about the gods and participating in them through theurgic practice. The one leads to theology; the other leads to theosis. Corbin placed himself firmly in the second tradition, and his scholarship was itself aform of participation.

body.caption: The Angel Gabriel — paradigm of the active Imagination.

body.text: Corbin'smeeting with Suhrawardi'stext in 1928 was the decisive event of his intellectual life. He described it as the moment his spiritual destiny was sealed, and he spent the following decades elaborating the vision that opened for him then — a Platonism expressed through the angelology of ancient Persia, encountered through the mystical traditions of Islam.

Corbin was born in Paris in 1903 and came into contact with Louis Massignon, Director of Islamic studies at the Sorbonne. When Massignon gave him avolume of the 12th-century Persian mystic Suhrawardi, Corbin wrote: 'through my meeting with Suhrawardi, my spiritual destiny for the passage through this world was sealed. Platonism, expressed in terms of the Zoroastrian angelology of ancient Persia, illuminated the path that Iwas seeking.' He was also studying the Protestant theologians and the hermeneutics of Martin Heidegger. In 1939 he went to Istanbul and Teheran for seven years to immerse himself in Islamic mysticism. He attended the Eranos conferences along with Carl Jung, Mircea Eliade and Gershom Scholem. It was in the prophetic mysticism of the Sufi tradition that Corbin found the central inspiration for his life work: the integration of Hermetic and neoplatonic gnosis into aspiritual path of the imagination.

The cosmology that underlies everything Corbin wrote is athree-tiered universe shaped by Neoplatonic emanation: the divine intelligible realm, the material realm, and between them amediating dimension that partakes of both. This middle realm is the mundus imaginalis, and it communicates itself to human beings through images that carry both sensory form and immaterial meaning. The figures that appear in this realm — gods, angels, daimons — are personifications of forces that are not merely psychological but ontologically real.

Neoplatonic cosmology consists of three planes of reality: the divine intelligible realm, the material realm, and linking them amediating dimension which partakes of both, aspiritual body or celestial earth. The reality of this place communicates itself to human beings through images, for images are perceived through sense-perception but may also carry an immaterial or divine meaning which appeals to the intellect. These planes are not static strata but dynamic intensifications of the energy of the supreme Divine Being which perpetually pours out through creation, and in neoplatonic terms, expresses itself in the middle realm through personifications we call gods, angels or daimons.

The imagination, in this framework, is the faculty that perceives the middle realm. Angels have no physical senses, so their perception is through imagination alone, and when human beings cultivate the active imagination they are developing the same mode of perception. Angel, soul and world become modes of perception rather than separate entities: the world is perceived through sense, soul through imagination, and angel through intellect. The act of imagining is itself adivine act of reconnection.

The celestial souls are empowered by the desire to return to their particular archangel. Each level of the material cosmos resonates with its soul and thereby with its angel, but the soul or angel of the world is only perceptible through the cultivation of what Corbin terms the active imagination. The imagination is the angelic mode of perception, for being immaterial they do not possess the sense-perception of human beings. Angel, soul and world are not separate entities out there, but modes of perception: the world is perceived through sense, soul through imagination and angel through intellect. As the imagination conveys divine thoughts through images, the very act of imagining becomes adivine act of reconnection.

body.caption: Corbin's vision: Platonism expressed through angelology.

body.text: Corbin'sown essay on the mundus imaginalis describes this interworld as the place where visionary revelation occurs — events that are experienced as more vividly real than everyday reality. The active Imagination guides and molds sense perception, transmuting sensory data into symbols. The Burning Bush is abrushwood fire if perceived only by the senses; for Moses to perceive it as theophany and hear the Voice calling him, an organ of trans-sensory perception is required.

In his essay 'Mundus Imaginalis,' Corbin gives adetailed exposition of this interworld as aplace of visionary revelation and events that are experienced as more vividly real than everyday reality. The active Imagination guides, anticipates, molds sense perception, that is why it transmutes sensory data into symbols. The Burning Bush is only abrushwood fire if it is merely perceived by the sensory organs. In order that Moses may perceive the Burning Bush and hear the Voice calling him, an organ of trans-sensory perception is needed.

The same object can be perceived through different faculties, and what it becomes depends on the faculty brought to it. The active Imagination couples objective and subjective worlds, literal fact and spiritual meaning. Directed toward matter it produces images of the kind we call fantasy; directed toward archetypal realities it becomes an organ of vision. The difference is one of direction alone.

body.caption: The three-tiered cosmos: intelligible, imaginal, material.

body.text: Thus the active imagination couples objective and subjective worlds, literal fact and spiritual meaning. When directed towards archetypal realities, it can bring these realities into harmony and resonance with the world, but if it is solely directed downwards towards matter it can only produce images which are fantastic, imaginary, unreal or even absurd. The task of human beings is to purify and liberate the soul so that it may begin to pick up the traces of divine meaning behind the appearances of things.

The Angel is the supreme form of manifestation in this tradition, and the encounter with the Angel is the goal of the spiritual path. Each human soul has acelestial counterpart, a Perfect Nature or Guardian Angel, which is its eternal and perfected individuality. Corbin writes that the Angel is the face that God takes for each person, and that one finds God only when one recognises that face. This recognition takes place in the imaginal world.

The Angel is the supreme form of manifestation of Absolute Being in this tradition. Corbin says: 'The Angel is the face that our god takes for us, and each of us finds his god only when he recognises that face.' Such arecognition takes place in the imaginal world. According to this tradition of prophetic philosophy, the active intellect of God can only be encountered through the Angel of Revelation personified as an individual angelic being. It could be speculated upon as an abstract concept, but only fully understood through personal encounter.

body.caption: The mundus imaginalis as the place of visionary experience.

body.text: The integration of the earthly ego with its celestial counterpart is the central work of the spiritual life, and it is not accomplished through intellectual effort alone. It involves apainful purification, abreaking through the boundaries of habitual consciousness, and an opening to an intensity of existence normally inaccessible. The metaphors for this process recur across traditions: travelling to the underworld, fighting the dragon, undergoing the alchemical nigredo.

Each human soul has as its counterpart acelestial soul, who is the eternal and perfected individuality of the person, their transcendent celestial self. The question then becomes how to integrate the earthly ego with this soul and through it with its angel, for it is through such an engagement that the individual becomes fully a Person, an integrated whole, connected to the source of Being yet also active in the world. Making the connection is not easy; it involves breaking through the boundaries of habitual consciousness and opening up to an intensity of existence normally inaccessible.

Ta'wil is the hermeneutical method that facilitates this integration. It means restoring the true meaning of atext or image by transmuting the world into symbols, moving through the literal, the allegorical, the symbolic, and the anagogic levels of understanding. The crucial transition is from allegory to symbol, because allegory disguises what is already known while symbol discloses something irreducible that could be known in no other way. Allegory is harmless; symbol transforms the one who deciphers it.

body.caption: The Angel as the soul's celestial counterpart.

body.text: Ta'wil refers to the process of interiorisation, of restoring the true meaning of atext or image through transmuting the world into symbols. This is the hermeneutical method whereby asacred text reveals its central, mystical message through the stages of the literal, the allegorical, the symbolic and the anagogic levels of understanding. The crucial point is the transition from allegorical to symbolic interpretation, for it is here that the 'turning upside down' of reality occurs and the spiritual meaning becomes apparent. Corbin says: 'The current attitude is to oppose the real to the imaginary as though to the unreal, to confuse symbol with allegory. Every allegorical interpretation is harmless; the allegory is adisguising of something that is already known. But the appearance of an Image having the quality of asymbol is aprimary phenomenon, unconditional and irreducible.'

The power that drives this entire process is himma, which Corbin defines as the force of an intention so powerful that it can project and realise abeing external to the one who conceives it. The mystic'shimma operates through an intensity of imagination that resonates on the plane of archetypal Ideas, and what we call miracles are the result of this capacity to bring spiritual power to bear on matter.

Corbin defines himma as 'the act of meditating, conceiving, imagining, projecting, ardently desiring. It is the force of an intention so powerful as to project and realise abeing external to the being who conceives the intention.' The himma of amystic can create changes in the world through an intensity of imagination that resonates on the plane of archetypal Ideas; he is thus himself adivine creator who establishes the patterns from which material forms derive. What we call amiracle is the result of such acapacity to bring spiritual power to bear on matter.

body.caption: Ta'wil: spiritual hermeneutics as the soul's ascent.

body.text: The artist who perceives the Angel through himma and gives it form leads the viewer to also perceive it, because in the imaginal world to perceive something is to participate in its reality. Corbin suggests that the artist performs the role of magician, creating external forms through art for the divine quality perceived through himma, and in this way leads others to the same perception.

To those who have cultivated the direction of affective force through longing and desire, the Angel will reveal itself, often in an intensely private and uncommunicable way, disguised as an event which to an outsider seems no different from any other. Corbin suggests that the artist too may perform the role of magician, creating external forms through his art for the divine quality he has perceived through his himma, and thus leading the viewer or listener also to perceive it.

Voss places Corbin within the lineage of Platonic interpreters that runs from Plotinus and Iamblichus through to Jung and Hillman. The continuity of this tradition is that it takes visionary experience seriously as amode of knowledge, and Corbin'simportance is that he gave this tradition aprecise philosophical language at atime when the imagination had been reduced to fantasy. He reconnects the reader with the possibility of direct spiritual participation, demonstrating that the study of religious experience need not remain an objective historical exercise — it can become aliving reality for the one who undertakes it.

The importance of Corbin'swork cannot be overestimated in aworld which is blinkered and starved of asense of the sacred, and which tends to reduce the imagination to fantasy and illusion. He gives us alanguage in which to speak with penetrating insight about the reality of visionary experience, aplace to locate divinatory realisation. He reconnects the reader with what has become popularly known as 'the power of now' through demonstrating that afaithful study of religious experience must involve amove away from the objectifying approach of the historian, towards the position of the mystic for whom it is aliving reality.
